planting.note: Direct sow in fall into a prepared seedbed of lean, well-drained soil; seeds need a period of winter cold to break dormancy and will germinate naturally the following spring.

directions: Rose Pink is a native annual or biennial of eastern North American meadows, clearings, and open woodland edges, instantly recognized by its four-angled (winged) stems and intensely fragrant, vivid pink, five-petaled flowers that appear July through August. Scatter seeds in fall on the surface of lean, well-drained soil in partial to full sun; plants resent rich or heavily amended beds and will become floppy and bloom-shy in fertile garden soil. In its second year as a biennial, the plant produces a profuse flush of bloom, drawing bumblebees and long-tongued native bees before setting abundant seed and dying. Allow seed to fall in place each year to establish a self-perpetuating colony, as Rose Pink relies on natural reseeding rather than vegetative spread.

planting.note: Direct sow at last frost directly onto moist, sandy soil; seeds are tiny and should not be covered — simply press them into the soil surface and keep consistently moist until germination.

directions: Sea Pink is a native annual of Atlantic and Gulf coast salt marshes, tidal flats, and coastal meadows, producing bright pink flowers with a distinctive yellow-green starry center from July through September; it is one of the few Sabatia species truly tolerant of salt spray and brackish soils. Sow seeds directly onto moist, sandy or peaty soil in full sun at last frost, pressing the dust-fine seeds into the soil surface without covering them, and maintain consistent moisture during germination. It thrives in lean, acidic, poorly drained coastal soils that would kill most cultivated annuals and declines rapidly in rich or heavily amended garden beds. Sea Pink is an excellent choice for rain garden edges, coastal restoration plantings, and naturalistic saline meadows where its salt tolerance gives it a distinct competitive advantage.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring onto the surface of moist, sandy or peaty soil; seeds are tiny and light-dependent for germination — do not cover, and keep the seedbed consistently moist until plants are established.

directions: Bartram's Rosegentian is a Florida endemic perennial found in wet flatwoods, moist savannas, and seepage slopes, making it one of the largest-flowered members of its genus with vivid rose-pink blooms up to 1.5 inches across appearing in summer and fall. It requires consistently moist to seasonally wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soils in full sun — conditions that mimic its natural flatwoods habitat — and will not tolerate prolonged drought or alkaline garden soils. Sow seeds on the surface of moist, lean growing media in spring without covering, as the fine seeds require light for germination; transplant young seedlings carefully once they reach an inch tall, as the taproot is sensitive to disturbance. This species is an important component of Florida flatwoods wildflower restorations and is a significant nectar source for native bees and butterflies during its long summer-to-fall bloom period.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into moist, acidic soil; seeds require cold-moist stratification over winter and will germinate the following spring — do not cover seeds, as light aids germination.

directions: Elliott's Gentian is a native perennial of southeastern wet savannas, pitcher plant bogs, and seepage slopes, producing some of the most intensely colored violet-blue tubular flowers of any eastern wildflower during September through November — often the very last wildflower blooming before hard frost. It is genuinely difficult to cultivate and requires close replication of its native habitat: consistently moist to wet, highly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), nutrient-poor, sandy or peaty soil in partial sun, as well as the presence of specific mycorrhizal fungal associates in the soil that the plant depends on for long-term survival. Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in fall without covering them, as they need cold stratification and light for germination; expect very slow seedling growth, with plants typically requiring two or more years to reach blooming size. This species is best established from seed directly into a naturalistic savanna or bog restoration rather than in a conventional garden setting, as transplanting is difficult and container-grown specimens rarely persist without their native fungal partners.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into sandy, well-drained soil; seeds benefit from natural winter cold stratification and will germinate in early spring when soil temperatures rise above 60°F.

directions: Sand Blue-eyed Grass is a delicate, grass-like native perennial of the southeastern coastal plain, producing small but jewel-like violet-blue flowers with a bright yellow center from spring through early summer atop flat, iris-family stems that blend naturally into sandy meadows and open pinelands. Despite its common name, it is not a true grass but a member of the iris family (Iridaceae), and its fine, strap-like foliage forms low, slowly spreading clumps that are nearly invisible when not in bloom. It thrives in dry to moist, lean, sandy or gravelly soils in full to partial sun and strongly resents heavy clay, poor drainage, or high-fertility garden beds where competing vegetation quickly overtops it. Sow seeds in fall by scattering them on the surface of sandy soil, allowing winter cold to break dormancy naturally; established clumps can also be gently divided in fall to expand a planting.
